content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916)322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
hlly 10,2002
Gary T. Ragghianti
Ragghianti, Freitas, Macias
& Wallace
874 Fourth Street, Suite D
San Rafael, C A 94901 -3246
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-170
Dear Mr. Ragghianti:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember
Lawrence Lanctot regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").I
QUESTION
Does Councilmember Lanctot have a disqualifying conflict of interest under
regulation 18704.2, which prevents him from participating in the Larkspur City Council
decisions concerning adoption of a specific plan and zubsequent related land use
determinations for the Niven property project in Larkspur?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Lanctot may participate in the specific plan decisions regarding the
Niven property development if, in fact, there will be no financial effect on his residential
property.
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of tlre California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-02-170
Page No. 2
FACTS
Lawrence R. Lanctot is an elected member of the Larkspur City Council. Mr. Lanctot
resides in a single-family residence in Larkspur, California. His residence is within 500 feet
of the southern boundary of the proposed Niven property project.
The Niven property consists of approximately l7 acres and is situated along Doherty
Drive and Ward Street in Larkspur. It was formerly used to conduct a nursery operation,
which has now terminated. The specific plan application mentioned herein is related to the
ultimate development and use of the property for the purposes described within this letter.
The Larkspur General Plan 1990-2010, adopted in December 1990, provides, in
pertinent part, that if, on the Niven property, there is "a change in land use to other than
existing nursery uses" a "specific plan shall be required before the property is redeveloped in
any use other than a nursery." (Larkspur General Plan pp. 35,39-40.) The general plan
"recommends" that any change in land use designation for the Niven property be for "low
density residential with higher density allowed for affordable housing" and indicates an
"approximate maximum development potential" of "90 units (or more if higher density
affordable housing is proposed)." (Larkspur General Plan, pp. 35,42.)
California land use law requires that whenever a clty adopts a specific plan, such plan
must be consistent with the provisions of any existing general plan adopted by the City of
Larkspur. (Gov Code $65454.)
The City of Larkspur appointed a citizens advisory committee to draft a proposed
specific plan for the Niven property as well as six (6) adjacent commercial and/or publicly
owned parcels in the downtown area. The committee referenced was known as the "CLASP"
(Central Larkspur Area Specific Plan) Committee. The CLASP Committee prepared its
"Central Larkspur Specific Plan" ("CLASP report") for these seven (7) parcels as a
non-binding recommendation to be considered by the Larkspur City Council for adoption, in
whole or in part, or alternatively for modification or rejection.
The 17 acre Niven parcel's southern boundary abuts the Larkspur Creek which is
owned by the City of Larkspur. On the southern edge of the Larkspur Creek is a public
pathway near the backyards of a number of single family residences fronting on the street on
which Councilmember Lanctot resides. However, Mr. Lanctot's residence does not abut the
southern edge of the Larkspur Creek and is further distant from the Niven property.
The CLASP report makes separate recommendations for each of seven (7) identified
parcels. For the Niven parcel, the CLASP report recommends that the Niven property have a
fifty foot (50') creekside open space corridor measured northerly from the northern edge of
the Larkspur Creek toward the interior of the Niven parcel.
The CLASP report's proposed land use recommendation is for residential housing
consisting of a range (23-33) of cottage homes, a range of single family residences (25-35),
and affordable housing for up to 27 low to moderate affordable housing units. If affordable


File No. A-02-170
Page No. 3
housing is provided, the total number of market rate collage and single-family homes may
not exceed 58, but if such affordable housing is not provided, the total number of market rate
units may be 63. Other land use recommendations contained in the report include the open
space creekside corridor described above and a public park dedication in a size as required by
existing Larkspur ordinances. The land uses are limited to the residential, open space and
attendant roads, with exception for possible limited (4,000 square feet) retail space near an
adjacent commercial parcel, public parking, and community serving space for the residents of
the Niven property.
The CLASP report discusses other matters including circulation, recommending
primary access to the Niven property on Doherty Drive and a road system which discourages
through traffic between Ward Street and Doherty Drive, plus various bike and pedestrian
paths on the Niven property. The report also discusses community design concerns including
standards for building, road and landscape design, and also discusses utilities and storm
water drainage, including existing water mains and sewers, which are sufficient to service all
seven CLASP parcels.
Mr. Lanctot engaged Mr. Norman Nowicki, an experienced certified real estate
appraiser with Appraisal Research Corporation, to investigate, using criteria specified in
regulation 18705.2(b)(l)(A)-(C), whether Councilmember Lanctot's voting on the subject
matter could reasonably have any financial effect on his interest in the residential real
property he owns. Mr. Nowicki's appraisal was to evaluate the effect of the proposed Niven
development, as described in the CLASP report, on the value of Mr. Lanctot's residential
property.
Mr. Nowicki's appraisal of June 28,2002, after inspecting Mr. Lanctot's residence
and the immediate and surrounding neighborhoods in Larkspur, and after taking into account
the applicable facts and circumstances described by regulati on 18705.2. concluded that
approval of the development of the Niven property and related parcels set forth in the
CLASP report consistent with the Larkspur General Plan provisions will not have any
financial effect on the value of Mr. Lanctot's residential property
In reaching his conclusion, Mr. Nowicki advises that he considered the following
matters: that Mr. Lanctot's residence is not income producing and that as one home in a
single family neighborhood, his residence will retain its current character for the foreseeable
future. He took into account the proposed access to the Niven property, and the factthat
egress from and ingress to Mr. Lanctot's residence can be accomplished other than by the
proposed access roads (Doherty Drive and limited Ward Street access) to the Niven property.
He determined that the Meadowood neighborhood in which Mr. Lanctot's home is situated is
separated from the Niven property, physically and topographically. He noted that the views
from Mr. Lanctot's residence are oriented to the west towards Mount Tamalpais and not to
the north in the direction of the Niven property, and that any views to the Niven property
from the front of his residence are blocked by other homes and tall trees and landscaping. He
determined that both Mr. Lanctot's residence and his neighborhood will not be substantially
affected by increased levels of noise, traffic, air or any other significant factors resulting from
development of the Niven property.


File No. A-02-170
Page No. 4
Mr. Nowicki has been engaged as a qualified appraiser for more than 40 years and
regularly performs real property appraisals throughout the Bay Area.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public oflicial from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the prohibition in
section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides an eight-step analysis.
(1) and (2) Is the individual a public oflicial and will he be making, participating in
making, using or attempting to use his/her oflicial position to influence a government
decision?
As a council membeq Mr. Lanctot is a public official. He wishes to make and
participate in his official capacity in the city's decision regarding the specific plan for the
Niven property.
(3) Identify the official's economic interests.
Under $ 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests that may
result in a conflict of interest for a public official. The one identified in your advice request
is Mr. Lanctot's home, which is:
Real Property: A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair market
value. (Section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2.) We assume that Mr. Lanctot's interest in his
home is worth more than $2,000. Thus, the council member's real property is an
economic interest under the Act.
(4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision which the public official
will be making, participating in making, using or attempting to use his/her oflicial
position to influence.
Real property is directly involved in a decision if it is the subject of a governmental
decision or if any part of the public offrcial's real property is located within 500 feet of the
real property which is the subject of the -governmental decision (Reg lS70a.2@)(l);
Cosgrove Advice Letter No. I-92-530).)2 Since Mr. Lanctot's property is within 500 feet of
the Niven property, the subject of the specific plan decision, the council member's property
is directly involved in the decision.
2 Regulation 18704.2 (a)(1)-(5) enumerates several factors to determine if the real property is the "subject of
the governmental decision." However, please note that the Commission does not consider this an exclusive list.
In this regard, the Commission will be looking at clariffing amendments to this regulation at its September 5,
2002, meeting to show that the 500 foot test is not limited to the factors listed in regulation 18704.2(a)(l)-(5).


File No. A-02-170
Page No. 5
Steps (5) and (6): Will the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
After determining a public official's economic interest, it must be decided whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on any of
those economic interests. First, the applicable standard of materiality must be found. Then it
must be determined if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the decision will reach the
materiality threshold. Regulation 18706 states: "[a] material financial effect on an economic
interest is reasonably foreseeable ... if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards [citation] applicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of
the governmental decision. "
The materiality standard for real property directly involved in a governmental
decision is in regulation 18705.2(a)(l):
"Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a
governmental decision on the real property is presumed to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial
effect on the real property."
Because there is a presumption that financial effects on real property directly
involved in a governmental decision are material, a material financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable in such decisions. In order to rebut the presumption of materiality, Mr. Lanctot
must prove that there will be no financial effect on his real property interest, not even one
penny, resulting from the decision.3
A public official may seek third-party assistance, such as areal estate appraisal, to
help decide whether the presumption holds true in his or her situation. We have advised in
the context of indirectly involved real property that an appraisal conducted by a disinterested
and otherwise qualified real estate professional, that is based upon an accurate understanding
of the underlying facts, and that also considers the factors listed in regulation
18705.2(b)(l)(A)-(C), will generally be considered as a good faith effort by a public official
to assess the financial effect of a decision on his or her property. (Perkins Advice Letter, No.
A-e9-024.)
However, a public official may not simply rely on a third-party appraisal without
further inquiry into whether the person conducting the appraisal is qualified to do so, whether
the appraisal considered the appropriate standards and all ofthe appropriate factors described
in our regulations, and whether the conclusion reached by the appraiser is objectively
defensible (e.g., based on a fulI and accurate assessment of the underlying facts). (O'Harra
3 This is a strict standard (the so-called "one-penny rule"). If ttre decision can reasonably be foreseen as having
a financial effect as litfle as even one permy upon a public offrcial's directly involved economic interest in real
propefty, it will, inthe absence of an exception, be cause for disqualifying the public official from involvement
in the decision.


File No. A-02-170
Page No. 6
Advice Letter, A-00-174.) When these criteria are met, a public offrcial may find that a
third-party appraisal provides a reasonable and objective basis for rebutting the presumption
that a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon his or her
economic interest in directly involved real property.
In this situation, Mr. Nowicki concluded in his appraisal that"the proposed Niven
development will not have any financial impact on values in the immediate neighborhood nor
on the value of Mr. Lanctot's residence." The appraisal listed several factors to support his
conclusion, as noted on page 3 of this letter. He also stated that he reviewed regulation
18705.2 in preparing his appraisal.
Therefore, based on the above information, if Mr. Lanctot concludes, pursuant to the
appraisal, that there will be no financial effect on his property, he may participate in the
specific plan decisions regarding the Niven property.a
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(et6)322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
itL
By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division
JS:jg
I:\Adviceltrs\02-170
a Please note that the Commission caffiot make the determination of materiality which is necessarily a factual
question. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact inproviding advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops.71.)

regulation 18704.2, which prevents him from participating in the Larkspur City Council

determinations for the Niven property project in Larkspur?

Councilmember Lanctot may participate in the specific plan decisions regarding the
Niven property development if, in fact, there will be no financial effect on his residential
property.
